coredata.dc:title: Performance metrics for the comparison of lithium ion cell aging experiments
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Shenzhen City Clean Energy Research Institute, Shenzhen University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Shenzhen City Clean Energy Research Institute, Shenzhen University.
coredata.dc:creator: Heyer, Gabriel
D'Arpino, Matilde
Rizzoni, Giorgio
coredata.dc:description: The increasing use of lithium-ion cells in large-scale, long-term applications drives a need for design methods that considers aging and accurate state of health estimation. A common approach is to rely on an empirical or semi-empirical aging model fit to experimental data to estimate the evolution of capacity and power fade. Because aging data are costly to collect, pack designers either use Design of Experiment (DOE) techniques to define a set of efficient tests, or use existing aging data to calibrate aging models. Given the increasing quantity of available aging data, the question arises: how can experimental aging campaigns be quickly compared? However, a methodology for the comparison of sets of aging experiments is not discussed in the literature. As a result, pack designers usually rely on intuition to select between multiple aging studies proposed by DOE techniques or in the literature. This work proposes metrics to quantitatively capture the alignment between a set of aging experiments and a target application. These metrics allow pack designers to quickly compare many sets of aging experiments to evaluate those which have tested conditions relevant to the application. Case studies are presented to illustrate the application of these metrics using aging campaign data from the literature. To validate these metrics, this work examines the relationship between these metric values and aging model validation error for calendar aging data for 18650 NMC battery cells. It is demonstrated that greater metric values correspond to reduced model error for an empirical capacity fade model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Battery aging
Battery testing
Design of experiment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Current profile for the US ABC PHEV drive cycle (left) and resulting probability density function (right).

Application of α and β-metrics to the US ABC PHEV drive cycle considering the Preger 2020 dataset. The range of aging factor values tested is shaded, and the individual tests are shown in the circular markers.
Summary of metric values for the defined HEV profile (top) and BEV profile (bottom).
Flowchart description of the metric validation procedure.
Summary of k parameter dependence on temperature and state of charge for each dataset. When applicable, best fit lines are shown as dotted lines.
Probability density functions for each dataset.
Comparison of model performance and metric values for each dataset.

Summary of aging studies used in this work for calendar aging (top) and cycling aging (bottom). Table values denote the range of conditions tested, and the number of unique conditions tested (e.g., the number of different temperature conditions examined) are denoted in parenthesis. Note that only the data available in plots are included here, in some cases additional conditions were tested.
Considered factors and levels (top) and experiments (bottom).
Metrics for each of the DOE proposed studies.
Calibration parameter values for each model.

head.ce:title: Performance metrics for the comparison of lithium ion cell aging experiments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, The Ohio State University, 201 W 19th Ave, Columbus, OH, 43212, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, The Ohio State University, 201 W 19th Ave, Columbus, Ohio 43212

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Automotive Research, The Ohio State University, 930 Kinnear Rd, Columbus, OH, 43212, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Automotive Research, The Ohio State University, 930 Kinnear Rd, Columbus, Ohio 43212

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, The Ohio State University, 201 W 19th Ave, Columbus, OH, 43212, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing use of lithium-ion cells in large-scale, long-term applications drives a need for design methods that considers aging and accurate state of health estimation. A common approach is to rely on an empirical or semi-empirical aging model fit to experimental data to estimate the evolution of capacity and power fade. Because aging data are costly to collect, pack designers either use Design of Experiment (DOE) techniques to define a set of efficient tests, or use existing aging data to calibrate aging models. Given the increasing quantity of available aging data, the question arises: how can experimental aging campaigns be quickly compared? However, a methodology for the comparison of sets of aging experiments is not discussed in the literature. As a result, pack designers usually rely on intuition to select between multiple aging studies proposed by DOE techniques or in the literature. This work proposes metrics to quantitatively capture the alignment between a set of aging experiments and a target application. These metrics allow pack designers to quickly compare many sets of aging experiments to evaluate those which have tested conditions relevant to the application. Case studies are presented to illustrate the application of these metrics using aging campaign data from the literature. To validate these metrics, this work examines the relationship between these metric values and aging model validation error for calendar aging data for 18650 NMC battery cells. It is demonstrated that greater metric values correspond to reduced model error for an empirical capacity fade model.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion cells are an energy storage technology of interest in recent years due to their safety characteristics, compatibility with renewable energy sources, and favorable energy and power densities [1–4]. These cells are also used in large scale applications including electric vehicles [2,3], hybrid-electric aircraft [5,6], and stationary power applications [3,7,8]. However, a challenge arises when using lithium ion cells: the cells degrade due to both time and use. These phenomena are categorized as calendar aging and cycling aging, respectively [3,9,10]. The effects of aging can be observed in a reduction in the power and capacity of the cell. These effects are referred to as power fade and capacity fade, respectively [10–12]. As a result, the estimation of future cell aging is critical for many applications [13,14].

To satisfy this need, the effects of aging factors on the rate of cell aging are often captured using empirical/semi-empirical aging models [10]. These models are favored for system-level analysis and online aging estimation due to the reduced computational cost compared to physics-based aging models [2,8,13,15]. Cell aging is dependent upon many aging factors, including temperature and state of charge (SOC) for calendar aging, and temperature, cycle SOC range, or similarly depth of discharge (DOD), and C-rate for cycling aging [10,16]. Empirical/semi-empirical models seek to capture these relationships through the calibration of a model to experimental data. Because empirical aging models are not first-principle models, it is critical that the experimental conditions used to calibrate the model be similar to those expected in the target application (TA), as the extrapolation performance of empirical/semi-empirical aging models may not be reliable [17]. It is therefore ideally appropriate to conduct a large number of tests to ensure accurate model performance over a range of operating conditions. However, conducting many tests is both expensive and time consuming [13,14,18], and may not possible in the early design stages when multiple candidate cells are being considered. This leaves pack designers with two options: (i) use design of experiments (DOE) theory to construct a set of aging experiments which examine the effects of aging factors in an efficient manner, or (ii) use published aging experimental results to calibrate the aging model.

Each approach comes with a set of challenges. DOE allows for the selection of aging experiments given a set of aging factors and aging factor levels [10,14,16,19]. Although the relevant aging factors are well-documented in literature [12], there is less agreement regarding the effect of cycling aging factors compared to those for calendar aging [14]. The appropriate selection of levels depends on the TA and the type of experimental study, and is critical considering the limited extrapolation performance of empirical/semi-empirical aging models. Pack designers may also have to compare among various DOE study methods [10]. For example, many DOE study methods have been applied to the development of aging experiments, including Taguchi orthogonal designs [16], D-optimal designs [13], central-composite designs [14], and factorial designs. A comprehensive review of the application of DOE techniques to aging is presented in reference [10]. On the other hand, the use of published aging experiment results is cheaper, but offers pack designers no control over the test protocol, and the testing conditions may not align with the TA. Still, the desirability of access to open-source battery aging datasets has motivated reviews [18] and the creation of data repositories [20]. Reference [21] and reference [20] also provide detailed reviews of published aging experiments for calendar and cycling aging, respectively.

Given these background, two possible needs for the battery design community are envisioned here, considering both the use of DOE methodologies and existing aging data: (i) assessing the alignment of an existing data set to a specific TA, and (ii) assessing the alignment of a proposed aging study to a TA.

In this paper a tool that can support both needs is developed. It supports the first by offering a quantitative measure of the data coverage of the most common and most extreme application conditions, and the second by using the aforementioned metrics to compare proposed aging studies. Given that open-source aging data will become increasingly more available and there exists a need for the ability to systematically evaluate the alignment between a set of aging experiments and a TA, this ability would aid in the selection of published aging data for model calibration, the comparison of DOE study types, and in the selection of appropriate DOE aging factor levels. The objective of this work is to propose and validate metrics to fulfill this need. These metrics will allow for the quantitative comparison of multiple sets of aging experiments, generated from DOE theory or obtained from published results in literature, considering the alignment to a defined TA.

This section introduces the structure of aging experiments and the considerations that must be made when examining a set of aging experiments. A summary of aging data from literature is presented for use in case studies.

Aging experiments for lithium-ion cells are conducted by subjecting a cell to a fixed set of aging factor levels over an extended duration of time. The application of the aging factor levels will cause the cell to degrade. This degradation is periodically measured throughout the experiment with a reference performance test (RPT) [18]. RPTs often include a full cell discharge to measure capacity and current pulses or electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to measure resistance growth. By conducting additional experiments at different aging factor levels, the effects of each aging factor on the degradation of the cell can be explored. Ideally, all of the aging factors discussed in literature [12] would be tested at many factor levels. However, cost and time constraints must be considered when designing aging experiments [13,14,18]. The key properties of a set of aging experiments are therefore:
1.
The type of cell tested,
2.
The aging factors considered,
3.
The range of aging factor levels, and
4.
The number of aging factor levels.

The results of many aging studies can be found in the literature [2–4,11,20,22–30]. Considering a review of these, several of the most relevant aging studies are selected for case studies in this work. A summary of the aging studies used for the case study in this work is presented in Table 1 . The range of aging factor conditions are reported, along with the number of unique conditions tested. For example, the Baghdadi 2016 [26] calendar aging dataset investigates temperature values at 30 ​°C, 45 ​°C, and 60 ​°C, corresponding to a range of 30–60 ​°C and three unique conditions. The aging sets include the Ecker 2014 dataset [3], which tested a large number of SOC calendar aging conditions and a number of mean SOC and DOD conditions for cycling, with the limitation of a fixed temperature and discharge C-rate. The Keil 2016 dataset [25] which included extensive tests regarding the impact of cell SOC on calendar aging for multiple cell chemistries. The Baghdadi 2016 dataset includes calendar and cycling aging for two chemistries and includes HEV-type discharge profiles for cycling aging. This dataset includes high discharge C-rates to reflect this application, and uses a smaller SOC range to emulate charge sustaining (CS) behavior. The de Hoog 2017 [2] dataset also tests calendar and cycling aging, and includes a wide range of SOC and DOD conditions, with the limitation of a single discharge C-rate. The Schimpe 2018 dataset [27] includes both calendar and cycling aging tests and features good temperature resolution for each, but only considers full constant-current discharge cycles. The Preger 2020 [20] dataset tests cycling aging for multiple cell chemistries and includes a range of temperature, DOD, and discharge C-rate conditions. Finally, the Trad 2020 dataset [29] includes calendar and cycling aging tests with a range of aging factor levels for each. One limitation of the aging data available in literature is that many tests conduct cycling aging at constant current conditions that do not reflect those conditions in application. While some recent work has focused on the development of application-specific synthetic duty cycles [31,32], this work seeks to provide pack designers with a tool to select tests from constant current experiments. This contribution allows pack designers to best use existing aging data, or to select a set of aging experiments to conduct, rather than conducting synthetic duty cycle experiments, which may be cost and time prohibitive.
This section presents the motivation and definition of the aging study metrics which are used in this work. Considerations regarding the behavior of each metric are also discussed.

A qualitative assessment of aging studies can be conducted considering the range and number of conditions tested, and the number of cells tested. However, the definition of quantitative metrics will allow for a more rigorous comparison of aging studies. This section proposes several metrics that can be used to evaluate the applicability of candidate aging studies (created via DOE techniques, or available in literature) to a TA. We define α-metrics, which rely on a probabilistic representation of the TA operating conditions, β-metrics, which rely on the definition of the boundaries of TA operating conditions, and DOD/SOC specific metrics for cycling. In each case it is assumed that the pack designer has information regarding the operating conditions under which the cell will be used. Namely, that probability density functions of the expected operation and the range of possible operation is available. With this information, the metrics can be applied to many applications.

Fig. 1 shows a current profile for a plug-in hybrid electric vehicle (PHEV) application. Consider that a pack designer wishes to either select an existing aging campaign, or design a new aging campaign, which aligns with this application. That is, the current profile shown is the exact current profile that the cell will experience in operation. This assumption is made to allow for the creation of a probability density function (PDF) for this example. In practice, analysis of the range of possible operation will be necessary to define appropriate PDFs. Aging campaigns often test only constant current discharge rates, so it is not immediately clear which discharge C-rate tests are of most importance. A useful representation is the PDF of the drive cycle current rate profile as shown in Fig. 1. It should be noted that the PDF here is presented as a histogram. This allows for a smooth PDF despite the discrete current levels in the current profile. The selection of the histogram bins therefore has a significant impact on the PDF for this example. The bins are selected to be 1C wide. In practice, it would be preferable to have a broader range of application data to avoid these challenges. This representation yields several approaches that could be used to select aging campaign specifications or to design a set of aging experiments. First, the aging campaigns which capture the most common current conditions (i.e., those with the greatest PDF height) could be used. This approach relies on the assumption that the majority of aging occurs at the most common conditions. This approach is formalized by the definition of the α-metrics. An alternative approach is to assume that the majority of aging occurs at the most extreme conditions (i.e., the highest current rates, the tails of the PDF). This approach is formalized by the definition of the β-metrics. Both of these metrics are discussed in the respective subsections.

The α-metric formulation aims to capture the extent to which the aging campaign tests the most common TA operating conditions. Given a known TA PDF for a aging factor P(X∗), the α-metric calculation is given by Equation (1).
(1)
α
x
=
∑
i
P
(
X
∗
=
x
i
)

Where x
i is the value of aging factor x in the i-th unique test conducted in the aging campaign (i.e., duplicate tests at the same aging factor condition are not included in the α-metric calculation.) Note that the subscript attached to α denotes the considered aging factor. Although the value of an α-metric does not have a direct physical interpretation, a larger value indicates more experiments at common aging factor values.

Considering the US ABC drive cycle as an example TA and the Preger 2020 dataset, the α-metric can be interpreted graphically from Fig. 2 . The tests (conducted at discharge rates 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C and a charge rate of 0.5C) are shown in the red markers plotted on the PDF. The α-metric can be determined by taking the summation of the PDF heights corresponding to each test. A sample calculation is provided in Equation (2).
(2)
α
I , P r e g e r
=
∑ i
P
(
I ∗
=
I i
)
= P
( I = − 1 C )
+ P
( I = 0.5 C )
+ P
( I = 1 C )
+ P
( I = 2 C )
+ P
( I = 3 C )
=
0.0383 + 0.3898 + 0.2660 + 0.1619 + 0.0144 = 0.8704
Because several tests are conducted at common C-rate conditions, the Preger 2020 dataset scores well considering the α
I metric.

The α-metrics can also be extended to multiple aging factors, referred to as α ×-metrics. For two aging factors x, y with a TA joint PDF P(X∗, Y∗), the α ×-metric is given by Equation (3).
(3)
α
x × y
=
∑
i
P
(
X
∗
=
x
i
,
Y
∗
=
y
i
)
Where x
i , y
i are the values of aging factors x, y in the i-th unique test conducted in the aging campaign. A limitation of these metrics is that the majority of cell aging may not occur at the most common aging factor values; it is possible that a significant portion of aging occurs at the most extreme (and less common) aging factor values.

The β-metric formulation aims to address the drawback of the α-metrics by measuring the extent to which the aging campaign captures the range of TA operating conditions. Intuitively, the β-metric is the proportion of the range of aging factor values in the TA which are tested by the aging campaign. The β-metric calculation is given by Equation (4). Note that the subscript attached to β denotes the considered aging factor and R is the range function, defined by Equation (5). A value of 1 corresponds to the entire TA range being contained in the experimental condition range.
(4)
β
x
=
R
(
X ∩
X
∗
)
R
(
X
∗
)
(5)
R
(
X
)
= max
(
X
)
− min
(
X
)
Where X∗ is a random variable with behavior mirroring that of the aging factor x in the TA. X is a random variable indicating the values of x tested in the aging campaign.

Considering the US ABC drive cycle example and the Preger 2020 dataset, the β-metric can be interpreted graphically from Fig. 2. The range of aging factor values captures by the tests are highlighted in orange. It can be observed that these tests cover less than half of the current rates in application, and correspond to a β-metric value of β
I ​= ​0.32. This result indicates that despite the high α
I metric score, the Preger 2020 dataset does not cover a sufficient range of C-rate conditions to be suitable for this TA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cycling metrics considering state of charge
ce:section.ce:para: While the α- and β-metrics are suitable for aging factors which remain at a nominal condition during aging experiments, it is not suitable for those which operate over a range of values. Although SOC during cycling is the most common of these, the metrics discussed in this section can be extended to other aging factors.

Considering a desired SOC window in a TA, several criteria can be used to select aging campaigns:
1.
Does the SOC window align with that in the TA (captured by the γ ΔSOC metric)?
2.
Does the DOD align with that in the TA (captured by the γ
DOD metric)?
3.
Does the average SOC align with that in the TA (captured by the
γ
S O C
̄
metric)?
Note that the latter two of these considerations are components of the first. SOC window alignment required both alignment of DOD and average SOC.

The first of these metrics is the ΔSOC metric, denoted as γ ΔSOC for clarity between the metric and the SOC window over which the cell operates. The γ ΔSOC metric measures the extent to which the SOC window in the TA aligns with that tested by the aging campaign. The calculation of the ΔSOC metric is given in Equation (6). Note that Equation (6) considers the maximum across the tests in the aging campaign; the metric score is determined by the single test that aligns best with the TA.
(6)
γ
Δ S O C
=
max
i
2 R
(
S O
C
i
∩ S O
C
∗
)
− R
(
S O
C
i
∪ S O
C
∗
)
R
(
S O
C
∗
)

Where SOC∗ is a random variable representing the SOC in the target application, SOC
i is a random variable representing the SOC in the i-th aging campaign test, and R is the range function. Intuitively, the ΔSOC metric is the normalized difference between the SOC window overlap and the non-overlapping range of the SOC windows. A value of 1 corresponds to a perfect SOC window overlap.

The DOD metric, similarly denoted as γ
DOD , measures the extent to which the experimental DOD window aligns with that in the TA. The γ
DOD metric calculation is shown in Equation (7). Finally, the
S O C
̄
metric
(
γ
S O C
̄
)
measures the alignment between the mean experimental SOC and that in the TA. The calculation of the
γ
S O C
̄
metric is given by Equation (7).
(7)
γ
DOD
=
max
i
1 −
| D O
D
i
− D O
D
∗
|
D O
D
∗
(8)
γ
S O C
̄
=
max
i
1 −
|
S O C
̄
i
−
S O C
̄
∗
|
S O C
̄
∗

DOD
i is the experimental DOD in the i-th test in the aging campaign and DOD∗ is the DOD window magnitude in the TA.
S O C
̄
i
is the experimental mean SOC in the i-th test in the aging campaign and
S O C
̄
∗
is the mean SOC in the TA.

An important consideration is the range of values each aging metric can assume, along with the physical interpretation of each. The β ​− ​metrics are the fraction of the TA aging factor range which overlaps with the range of tested conditions, and are bounded by the range [0,1]. Similarly, the DOD and
S O C
̄
metrics are a normalized error subtracted from unity. These metrics are also bounded by the range [0,1]. The ΔSOC metric is also an error subtracted from unity, but the normalization with respect to the range of SOC values in the TA allows this error to exceed unity. A lower bound of zero is applied to these metrics. On the other hand, the α ​− ​metrics are a summation of probability density function heights, which vary with changes in the TA operation range. As a result, the α ​− ​metrics lack physical interpretation, aside from a greater metric value corresponding to a greater number of tests at common operating conditions for a given application. Rather than apply normalization which is arbitrary and not physically-motivated to these values, the metric values are allowed to assume values on the range [0,∞].

This section presents the methods used to study the proposed metrics. The methodology used to conduct case studies to illustrate the application of the metrics are provided in Section 3.3.1 and 3.3.2. The approach to validate applicability of the metrics is discussed in Section 5. Case studies are conducted to illustrate the application of the metrics to two use cases:
1.
The selection of published aging data for the calibration of an aging model.
2.
The comparison of aging studies proposed by different DOE methodologies.
The methods for each of these case studies are discussed in the appropriate subsections.

Sample TA profiles for BEV and HEV applications are presented in Table 2 . It is assumed that the HEV application uses the battery in charge sustaining mode, while the BEV application uses the battery over the majority of the possible SOC range. The maximum C-rate is also greater for the HEV application because a smaller battery pack is expected to be used. In each case it was assumed that all aging factors followed normal distributions and are independent. Consider that a pack designer has identified the candidate aging studies summarized by Table 1, and wishes to select aging studies relevant to each application. To demonstrate the use of the metrics for this scenario, a set of α
T , β
T , α
I , β
I ,
γ
Δ S O C
,
γ
DOD
,
γ
S O C
̄
metrics are computed for each cycling aging study using the aging factor levels tested in each study and the TA aging factor range and distribution given by Table 2. Note that a separate set of metrics is calculated for each TA. This allows for the applicability of each aging study to each TA to be evaluated. It should also be noted that all of the PDFs for this example are assumed to be normal and independent. However, for many applications this may not be true; greater current values may be associated with higher temperatures. This would affect the behavior of the α × metrics, as the joint PDF would increase near the high temperature/high current conditions. This would not affect the analysis procedure or metric computation, but would only require the appropriate joint PDF definition. Furthmore, the metrics can be applied to any application, only requiring the appropriate PDF and range of operation. The results of this analysis are presented in Section 4.

Using the HEV profile in Table 2, the factors and levels proposed in Table 3 are considered. Two DOE-informed studies are then considered, summarized in Table 3. These include a L4 orthogonal design and a full factorial design. The aging metrics are calculated for each of the proposed studies to evaluate the benefits of the full factorial design, given the increased number of tests. The results of this comparison are summarized in Section 4.2.
This section presents the results of the case studies outlined in Section 3.3.

The resulting metrics for each of the datasets discussed in Section 2 are summarized in Fig. 3 . The α
T and β
T metric values are identical between the two applications due to the identical range of temperature operation. The Schimpe 2018 dataset performs best with regard to both of these metrics because the dataset includes the greatest range and number of tested temperature conditions. On the other hand, the Baghdadi 2016 and Ecker 2014 datasets do not perform well considering these metrics because these tested only high temperatures and a single temperature value, respectively. The Preger 2020 and Trad 2020 datasets perform well considering the α
I and β
I metrics for each application. Each tested multiple current rates in the range of 0.5C–3C. These datasets yield greater metric values considering the BEV application compared to the HEV application because the testing range aligns better with the BEV current range (1C-3C) compared to the HEV current range (1C-5C).

For the BEV profile, the Ecker 2014, de Hoog 2017 and Preger 2020 datasets perform best considering the γ ΔSOC and γ
DOD metrics. Each of these datasets considered a DOD window near 100%, which aligns well with the application. All of the datasets performed well considering the
γ
S O C
̄
metrics for each application, as all datasets tested a mean SOC condition near 50%. For the HEV profile, the Baghdadi 2016 dataset performs best on the γ ΔSOC metric because one of the tested SOC windows is identical to that in the TA. In this case the Ecker 2014, Baghdadi 2016, de Hoog 2017, and Preger 2020 datasets perform well on the γ
DOD metric because each includes a test at 20% DOD.

The metric values determined considering the HEV use profile in Table 2 and the DOE-informed aging experiments in Table 3 are summarized in Table 4 . It is observed that the two studies are identical with respect to all metrics except the α
T×I metric. This result is expected because the levels, and therefore range, of experiments for temperature, current, and DOD are identical between the two studies. The benefit of the full-factorial design is that the combinations of high and low levels and their cross-interactions are observed in more detail. This is reflected in the α
T×I metric score. Therefore, the metric analysis of the two studies suggests that the experiment designer should evaluate whether the doubling the testing cost is worth the added knowledge of temperature and current interactions for the particular application.

Although the defined metrics allow for an intuitive measure of the alignment between an aging campaign and a target application, this does not ensure that the data will produce an aging model with better performance. The relationship between aging metrics and aging model performance is therefore of interest. To investigate this relationship, the following steps are taken. First, several calendar aging datasets for 18650 format NMC cells are identified. These are the aforementioned Ecker 2014, Keil 2016, and Trad 2020 datasets, along with the Schmitt 2017 dataset [33]. The data for evolution of capacity fade with regard to time are extracted using WebPlotDigitizer [34]. Next, aging models of the form given by Equation (9) are calibrated to each dataset. This model is inspired by those proposed in Refs. [11,22]. The extrapolation performance of each model is evaluated on the other datasets, thereby emulating a target application consistent with that dataset. Similarly, probability density functions are created to represent these target applications. This allows for the computation of aging metrics between the calibration dataset and the emulated target application. The relationship between the aging model performance and the dataset metrics can then be examined. This validation process is illustrated in Fig. 4 . Note that the figure only shows the validation process considering the Ecker 2014 dataset as the emulated TA. The process is repeated, using each of the other datasets as the emulated TA.
(9)
Q
loss
= k ⋅ exp
−
E
a
R ⋅ T
⋅
t
z
where Q
loss is the relative capacity loss of the cell, k is a parameter which is expected to be a function of temperature and SOC, E
a is the activation energy considering an Arrhenius temperature dependence, R is the ideal gas constant, T is the cell temperature, and t is the elapsed time in days. z is a calibration parameter which is expected to be near 0.5, assuming that aging is dominated by diffusion-limited solid electrolyte interphase growth [35].

To calibrate each aging model, the functional dependence of k on SOC and temperature is first investigated. Several temperature and SOC conditions are selected from each dataset, and the optimal k parameter values are determined for each condition using the fminsearch MATLAB command. The optimization is performed assuming a fixed E
a activation energy for each condition, a fixed z ​= ​0.5, and a sum-of-squared-errors cost function. The resulting k dependencies are shown in Fig. 5 .

For each dataset, a clear dependence on temperature can not be established. However, a linear dependence on SOC is observed in the Keil 2016 and Schmitt 2017 datasets An exponential dependence is observed in the Ecker 2014 dataset, noting the logarithmic axis scale in Fig. 5. Given these observations, the model forms for each data are summarized in Equations (10)–(13).
(10)
Q
ecker
= exp
a ⋅ S O C + b
⋅ exp
−
E
a
R ⋅ T
⋅
t
z
(11)
Q
keil
=
c ⋅ S O C + d
⋅ exp
−
E
a
R ⋅ T
⋅
t
z
(12)
Q
schmitt
=
c ⋅ S O C + d
⋅ exp
−
E
a
R ⋅ T
⋅
t
z
(13)
Q
trad
= f ⋅ exp
−
E
a
R ⋅ T
⋅
t
z

A final calibration step is then performed to identify the a, b, c, d, f, E
a , and z parameters for each model, as applicable. This calibration step uses the full data from each dataset. The calibration is again performed using the fminsearch MATLAB command and seeks to minimize the sum-of-squared-error. The resulting parameter values are summarized in Table 5 .

To compute the metric values between a calibration and validation dataset, PDFs representative of the validation dataset must be created. For each of the datasets, the height of the PDF at a given value is taken to be proportional to the number of tests conducted within 5% SOC or 5 ​°C of the value. The height is therefore given by Equation (14).
(14)
P
(
x =
x
∗
)
=
n
10 ⋅ N
Where N is the total number of tests and n is the number of tests within 5% SOC or 5 ​°C. The value of 10 in the denominator ensures that the area under the PDF is 1. The PDF height is also adjusted to avoid non-physical SOC values. The joint PDFs are computed in an equivalent manner, considering the number of tests with both a temperature and SOC condition within 5 ​°C and 5% SOC, respectively. The PDFs are shown in Fig. 6 . The α and β ​− ​metrics are computed as discussed in Section 3.

Fig. 7 presents the comparison between aging model performance and metric values for each of the datasets. Considering the Ecker 2014 validation results, a clear downward trend is established between each metric value and the model RMSE. This suggests that the datasets with greater metric values shared better alignment with the validation dataset. The Keil 2016, Schmitt 2017, and Trad 2020 datasets also share this trend in the α
SOC , β
SOC , and α
T×SOC metrics. However, an inverted trend is observed in the β
T metrics for these three datasets. This suggests that the range of temperature conditions was not a critical consideration for the selection of aging data in this application. This interpretation also aligns with the calibration results; in each case a clear temperature dependence of the k calibration parameter can not be established. This indicates that the majority of the temperature effects are captured by the Arrhenius relationship. Therefore, the extrapolation of aging to other temperature conditions may be more reliable compared to extrapolation to other SOC conditions. This may also explain the lack of a clear trend between model error and the α
T metric considering the Schmitt 2017 validation dataset.

The appropriate selection of aging data for the calibration of an empirical aging model is critical to the accuracy of the model. This work proposes several metrics which can be used to assess the applicability of an aging campaign to a target application. These metrics compare the tested conditions to the target application in an intuitive manner. Several datasets from literature are also examined and compared using these metrics. It is observed that the metrics highlighted the strengths and weaknesses of these datasets. Furthermore, these metrics are validated by comparing the metric values with aging model validation error. It can be observed that greater metric values correspond to reduced model validation error. This study illustrates how pack designers can select existing aging data or candidate sets of aging experiments to best align to a target application. These metrics provide pack designers with a methodology which allows for the selection of aging data which has been demonstrated to yield better model performance for 18650 NMC calendar aging. A limitation of this work is that these metrics do not account for the specific cell tested or the RPT protocol. This may mean that a dataset with a high metric score may not yield good extrapolation to another cell.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Gabriel Heyer: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - Original Draft. Matilde D'Arpino: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision. Giorgio Rizzoni: Conceptualization, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The Center for Automotive Research at The Ohio State University and the Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering at The Ohio State University.
